text: Three abdominal chokers are placed, a 12-millimeter number like this and two 5-millimeter in each flank.
A 10-millimeter 30-degree scope is used.
To improve the operating freeze exposure,
A pulchritinous suture is placed under the zygote process through the wrong ligament of the liver and fixed to the skin with calligraphers.
Another pulchritinous suture is passed under the right subcostal region through the mesentery of the gallbladder.
The procedure starts with cholecystectomy and the cystic duct and artery are divided between clips.
The gastric collar ligament is opened using ultrasonic shears and the transverse colon is taken down.
A third percutaneous suture is passed through the gastric antrum for retraction.
The gastrocolic trunk is put in evidence and it is sectioned between clips.
The second and third parts of the duodenum are mobilized and attachments are sectioned until the hepatic pedicle is reached posteriorly.
Another percutaneous suture is passed through the third part of the duodenum to improve the operator field's exposure.
The third part of the duodenum is freed using the ultrasound shears and the superior mesentery vein becomes evident.
Tributary veins are identified and sectioned between clips.
The first regional loop is found from lateral to medial and lymph nodes in the mesentery of this bowel segment are observed.
Hence, an approach to the bowel loop from the other side is considered.
The first bowel loop mesentery is sectioned staying below the observed nodes, using the coagulating hook, the ultrasonic shears, and clips.
The posterior attachments of the second part of the duodenum are freed as well, exposing the inferior vena cava as it goes towards the liver.
Lymphadenectomy at the hepatic pedicle is started, sectioning the tissue under the common bile duct, exposing the portal vein.
The peritoneum is opened at the portal hepatus, and the lymphadenectomy continues around the common bile ducts.
The dissection is then continued below the common bile ducts.
A piece of cotton tape is introduced into the abdomen and passed around the ducts.
The hepatic artery is located and the lymphadenectomy continues around this vessel.
This step is performed using the coagulating hook laterally and posteriorly.
Finally, the pars flaccida of the gastrohepatic ligament is sectioned.
The proper hepatic artery is identified with its bifurcation.
The right gastric artery is sectioned between clips.
The peritoneal sheath covering the pancreas is sectioned at the level of the body with the coagulating hook.
The lymphadenectomy of the ciliary trunk is started.
The splenic vein is exposed as well as the common hepatic artery.
The gastrododontal artery is approached from laterally and once denuded, it is sectioned between clips.
Lymphadenectomy is continued in the direction of the celiac trunk going around the common hepatic artery.
The dissection is then taken deeply behind the common hepatic artery.
Finally, the portal vein and the vessels of the celiac trunk are well exposed.
The lymph nodes are placed in a plastic bag.
In preparation for resection, the gastric mesentery at the lesser and greater curvatures of the stomach is opened.
The retropancreatic tunnel above the superior mesenteric vein is prepared.
A piece of cotton tape is passed.
The greater momentum is freed and the level for pancreatic resectioning is identified.
A 5 mm 30 degrees long scope is placed in the left flank port and an articulating linear stapler is placed in the umbilical 12 mm port.
The stomach is stapled and transected at the level of the atrium with the blue reload, 3.5 mm staples.
The jejunum is stapled and transected with the white reload, 2.5 mm staples.
The pancreas is closely encircled with the cotton tape and then transected with ultrasonic shears.
The pancreatic posterior attachments are free, staying lateral to the superior mesenteric vein and artery using the ultrasonic shears.
The common barrier duct is temporarily clamped.
The duct is transected using scissors.
The specimen is removed through a suprapubic access under protection for frozen section analysis.
The procedure continues with the reconstructive portion.
The pancreatic odigenostomy is firstly performed using a completely hands-on end-to-side method.
PDS20 is introduced through the umbilical port
and a running suture between the pancreatic parenchyma and the jejunal segment is performed.
Different bites are taken until the inferior corner is reached.
Once the inferior corner is reached, a new running suture starting at the superior corner is begun.
The jejunum is then opened and the anterior anastomotic wall is fashioned using the new running suture.
The inferior anastomotic corner is secured using a posterior running suture.
The final aspect of this anastomosis is shown.
The hepatico-digital anastomosis is then performed.
A posterior running suture using PDS 4-0 is done, keeping the clamp closed.
The diginum is then opened using a coagulating hole.
The anterior layer is performed using a new suture.
Finally, the two sutures are tied together.
The clamp is removed and the leak test of this anastomosis is established automatically.
The gastroenteric anastomosis is realized using the end-to-side hand-sewn method.
The posterior running suture using PDS1 is firstly placed on the gastric stump.
The gestional loop is selected and confirmed to be reaching the stomach easily.
Multiple bites are performed.
A new running suture is started at the same corner.
Both viscera are scored at their extremities.
Once the viscera are open, the corner of the anastomosis is cured and an orogastric bougie is temporarily passed into the jejunum.
Running bites are then taken to close the anterior wall of the anastomosis.
Finally, the oral gastric bulge is removed and a nasogastric tube is placed for the post-operative course.
Two abdominal drains are left close to the three anastomosis.
